title: Reading and Use of English – Part 5
description: You are going to read a magazine article about an intellectual process known as critical thinking. For questions 31 – 36, choose the answer (A, B, C or D) which you think fits best according to the text. Mark your answers on the separate answer sheet.

content.title: The Linguistic DNA: How Language Shapes Thought
content.subtitle: An exploration of the Sapir-Whorf hypothesis and the neurological boundaries of expression.
content.text: The relationship between the language we speak and the way we perceive the world has long been a battlefield for linguists and psychologists alike. At the heart of this debate is the concept of linguistic relativity, which suggests that the structural nuances of a mother tongue can influence, or even determine, the cognitive categories available to a speaker. [31] For decades, this idea was dismissed as overly deterministic, with critics arguing that human thought is a universal experience that transcends the specific vocabulary of any single nation. [/31] However, recent advancements in cross-cultural psychology have reignited interest in the theory, providing empirical evidence that our 'mental hardware' is more flexible than previously imagined. || Take, for example, the perception of color. Some languages, like Russian or Greek, have distinct words for 'light blue' and 'dark blue,' whereas English groups them under a single umbrella. [32] Experiments have shown that speakers of languages with these extra distinctions are significantly faster at discriminating between shades on a screen, suggesting that the brain's visual processing is literally sharpened by the labels it has been taught to apply. [/32] This does not mean that an English speaker cannot see the difference, but rather that their brain does not prioritize the distinction with the same neurological urgency. It is a subtle but profound shift in the hierarchy of perception. || The influence of language extends far beyond the visual spectrum and into the realm of spatial orientation. Some Aboriginal Australian communities do not use relative terms like 'left' or 'right,' relying instead on absolute cardinal directions—north, south, east, and west—for everything from describing a path to pointing out a speck of dust on a leg. [33] The result is a preternatural sense of direction that leaves Westerners baffled; even in a windowless room, a toddler from such a community can point toward the southeast without a moment's hesitation. [/33] Here, the language necessitates a constant, subconscious monitoring of one’s position in the world, effectively hardwiring a compass into the speaker's mind. || [34] Critics of linguistic relativity often point to the 'Translatability Argument,' suggesting that because we can explain any concept in any language—even if it takes more words—the underlying thought must be the same. [/34] While technically true, this ignores the 'cognitive cost' of such explanations. A concept that is encoded into a single, punchy word is handled differently by the brain than one that requires a laborious sentence. Language acts as a cognitive shorthand, and the specific shortcuts available to us determine which paths our thoughts are most likely to take. We are not prisoners of our language, but we are certainly its frequent travelers, following the well-worn trails it provides. || This brings us to the fascinating intersection of language and time. Some languages use spatial metaphors that place the future in front of the speaker, while others, like Aymara, place it behind. [35] The logic is that the past is known and therefore 'visible' (in front), while the future is unknown and 'invisible' (behind). [/35] This is not merely a linguistic quirk; it influences how these speakers gesture when talking about history and how they conceptualize the passage of life. It challenges the Western assumption that our way of 'viewing' time is the only logical or natural one. || Ultimately, the study of linguistic diversity is a study of human potential. [36] It suggests that the 'universal human mind' is not a single, finished product, but a versatile set of tools that can be configured in an astonishing variety of ways. [/36] As globalization threatens to extinguish minor languages at an alarming rate, we are losing more than just words; we are losing unique ways of being and thinking. Each language is a repository of a specific cultural wisdom, a unique lens through which the universe can be viewed. Protecting this diversity is not just a matter of cultural preservation; it is about keeping the full range of human cognitive possibilities alive for future generations.

questions.question: What does the writer suggest about linguistic relativity in the first paragraph?
options: A) It was initially rejected because it was considered too restrictive.
B) It fails to account for the flexibility of the human 'mental hardware'.
C) It has been widely accepted by linguists since its inception.
D) It is now considered the only valid way to explain human thought.
questions.explanation: B is supported by [31], which states the idea was 'dismissed as overly deterministic' (meaning too restrictive/fixed). A and C are contradicted by the text. D is the opposite of what is stated later in the paragraph.

questions.question: According to the second paragraph, how does language affect color perception?
options: A) It increases the speed at which the brain identifies specific color differences.
B) It makes color categorization an unconscious and urgent biological need.
C) It determines the physical structure of the speaker's eyes.
D) It prevents speakers of some languages from seeing certain shades.
questions.explanation: C is supported by [32], which notes speakers are 'significantly faster at discriminating between shades.' A is physiologically impossible. B is explicitly denied in the text ('does not mean... cannot see the difference'). D is an exaggeration.

questions.question: The author uses the example of Aboriginal Australian communities to illustrate...
options: A) the superiority of absolute cardinal directions over relative terms.
B) the difficulty of navigating without modern technology.
C) why toddlers are better at learning spatial terms than adults.
D) how language can foster a constant awareness of one's environment.
questions.explanation: C is supported by [33] and the following sentence, which mentions 'constant, subconscious monitoring' and 'hardwiring a compass.' A and D are not the main points. B is a value judgment the author doesn't explicitly make.

questions.question: What is the author's response to the 'Translatability Argument' in the fourth paragraph?
options: A) He suggests that the argument is irrelevant in the digital age.
B) He claims that most concepts cannot, in fact, be translated accurately.
C) He agrees that it proves the universality of human thought.
D) He argues that it underestimates the mental effort required for complex explanations.
questions.explanation: B is supported by the mention of 'cognitive cost' and the 'laborious sentence' [34]. A is incorrect because he qualifies his agreement. C and D are not discussed.

questions.question: The example of the Aymara language in the fifth paragraph highlights...
options: A) a linguistic system that is more logical than Western systems.
B) the way cultural metaphors can reverse standard perceptions of time.
C) the difficulty of gesturing while speaking about the future.
D) a common misconception about the visibility of the past.
questions.explanation: C is supported by [35], showing a reverse orientation of front/back compared to Western standards. A and B are not the author's claims. D is a literal misinterpretation of the gesturing mention.

questions.question: In the final paragraph, the writer implies that the loss of minor languages is...
options: A) an inevitable but manageable side effect of globalization.
B) a threat to the variety of ways humans are able to think.
C) primarily a concern for historians rather than scientists.
D) a sign that the 'universal human mind' is finally reaching a standard form.
questions.explanation: B is supported by [36], which argues we are losing 'unique ways of being and thinking' and 'cognitive possibilities.' A, C, and D contradict the author's concerned and urgent tone.
